name: Qdrant Local Mode
description: Qdrant Local Mode is an embedded, in-process implementation of the Qdrant vector search engine that runs entirely within the Python client with no external server required. It provides the full Qdrant API surface — collection CRUD, point insertion and management, vector similarity search with payload filtering, and hybrid search — using brute-force numpy-based search with optional SQLite-backed persistence. The same client code seamlessly transitions to Qdrant Server or Qdrant Cloud for production deployments. Local Mode is ideal for development, testing, prototyping, and small-scale datasets up to approximately 20,000 points per collection.
format_on_disk: SQLite-backed persistence (multi-file per storage directory) or ephemeral in-memory
core_strengths: Full Qdrant API surface in-process with zero external dependencies
Same client code transitions seamlessly to Qdrant Server or Cloud
Collection-based organization with vector similarity and payload filtering
Optional SQLite-backed persistence with file-locked exclusive access
Ephemeral in-memory mode (:memory:) for rapid testing and CI/CD
Supports dense, sparse, and multi-vector search with multiple distance metrics
best_use_cases: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) prototyping and local development
Unit and integration testing for Qdrant-based applications
Small-scale semantic search in local CLI and desktop tools
Learning the Qdrant API without deploying infrastructure
AI/ML experimentation with vector similarity search in notebooks
remarks: Local Mode is designed for datasets up to ~20,000 points per collection; for larger datasets, migrate to Qdrant Server or Qdrant Cloud. Uses brute-force search rather than HNSW indexing, so search performance scales linearly with collection size. Only one process may access a persistent storage directory at a time (enforced via portalocker). Install with `pip install qdrant-client` — no separate package needed for local mode. Local Mode uses numpy-based vector operations and supports Cosine, Dot, and Euclidean distance metrics.
